title: Demand and the law of demand
summary: This lesson introduces the fundamental concept of demand in economics. It covers the law of demand, the distinction between individual and market demand, and the critical difference between a movement along the demand curve (caused by a price change) and a shift of the entire curve (caused by non-price determinants).
learningObjectives: Define demand and explain the law of demand using the concepts of income and substitution effects.
Distinguish between individual and market demand, explaining how the latter is derived.
Explain the difference between a movement along the demand curve and a shift of the demand curve.
Analyse the non-price determinants of demand that cause the demand curve to shift.

simpleExplanation.title: Why We Buy More When Prices Drop
simpleExplanation.summary: Demand is all about how much of something people are willing and able to buy at different prices. The 'law of demand' simply states that when the price of a good falls, we tend to buy more of it, and when the price rises, we buy less.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think about your favourite takeaway pizza. If the price drops from £12 to £8 for a special promotion, you and your friends are much more likely to buy it this Friday night. If the price suddenly went up to £20, you'd probably choose something else. Your 'demand' for pizza changes based on its price and other factors, like how much money you have.
steps: First, understand that 'demand' isn't just wanting something; it's being willing AND able to buy it at a certain price.
Next, visualise the Law of Demand: as price falls, the quantity people want to buy increases. This creates a downward-sloping demand curve.

Then, distinguish between a 'movement' and a 'shift'. A price change for the good itself causes a movement along the existing curve. For example, a sale on trainers makes you move down your demand curve for them.
Finally, learn the 'shifters'. Factors other than the good's own price, like a change in your income or the price of a related good, will shift the entire demand curve to the left or right.

flashcards.back: The quantity of a good or service that consumers are willing and able to purchase at various prices during a specific time period, ceteris paribus.
The desire for a good or service backed by the ability to pay for it. This is the type of demand studied in economics, distinguishing it from just 'wanting' something.
States that, ceteris paribus, there is an inverse relationship between the price of a good and the quantity demanded. As price increases, quantity demanded decreases, and vice versa.
A graph illustrating the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity demanded. It is downward sloping from left to right.
The total quantity of a good or service demanded by all individual consumers in a market at each price. It is found by horizontally summing individual demand curves.
A change in the quantity demanded of a good that is the result of a change in that good's price. For example, moving from point A to point B on the same demand curve.
A change in the quantity demanded at every price, caused by a change in a non-price determinant of demand. The entire curve moves to the left (decrease) or right (increase).
A Latin phrase meaning 'all other things being held equal'. It's a key assumption in demand analysis, allowing us to isolate the effect of a single variable, like price.
A good for which demand increases as consumer income increases. For example, foreign holidays or branded clothing. It has a positive income elasticity of demand.
A good for which demand decreases as consumer income increases. For example, own-brand supermarket goods or bus travel. It has a negative income elasticity of demand.
Two goods that can be used in place of each other to satisfy a want. An increase in the price of one good leads to an increase in demand for the other (e.g., Coca-Cola and Pepsi).
Two goods that are consumed together. An increase in the price of one good leads to a decrease in demand for the other (e.g., smartphones and phone cases).

faq.q: What is the difference between 'demand' and 'quantity demanded'?
faq.a: This is a crucial distinction. 'Quantity demanded' refers to a specific amount of a good that consumers are willing to buy at one specific price. It is a single point on a demand curve. 'Demand' refers to the entire relationship between all possible prices and the corresponding quantities demanded. It is represented by the entire demand curve.

faq.q: Why is the demand curve downward sloping?
faq.a: The downward slope reflects the law of demand. It's due to three main factors: 1) The **income effect**: as price falls, your real income (purchasing power) increases, so you can afford to buy more. 2) The **substitution effect**: as price falls, the good becomes cheaper relative to its substitutes, so you switch from other goods to this one. 3) The **law of diminishing marginal utility**: as you consume more of a good, the extra satisfaction (utility) you get from each additional unit decreases, so you are only willing to buy more if the price is lower.

faq.q: Can a demand curve ever slope upwards?
faq.a: In theory, yes, but it is extremely rare in the real world. This would apply to a 'Giffen good', a highly inferior good where the income effect of a price increase is so strong that it outweighs the substitution effect, leading people to buy more as the price rises. For the IB SL course, you should assume demand curves are always downward sloping unless specifically told otherwise. Another exception is 'Veblen goods' (or luxury goods), where a higher price confers status, but this is more about a shift in preferences.

faq.q: If my income increases, will my demand for all goods increase?
faq.a: Not necessarily. For 'normal goods' (like restaurant meals or new technology), your demand will increase (shift right). However, for 'inferior goods' (like instant noodles or long-distance bus travel), your demand might decrease (shift left) as you switch to higher-quality alternatives that you can now afford.

sections.content: Welcome to the study of demand, a cornerstone of microeconomics. Why do we choose to buy certain products and not others? How does price influence our decisions? This lesson unpacks these questions, exploring the predictable patterns of consumer behaviour that drive market economies. Understanding demand is the first step to understanding how markets work, how prices are set, and how businesses make decisions.

In economics, 'demand' has a very precise meaning. It is not simply a want or a desire. Instead, demand refers to the quantity of a product that consumers are both **willing** and **able** to buy over a range of prices in a given period. This is often called 'effective demand'. If you would love to own a Ferrari but don't have the money, you do not have effective demand for it. The analysis of demand is almost always conducted under the *ceteris paribus* assumption, a Latin phrase meaning 'all other things being equal'. This allows us to examine the effect of one variable, such as price, while holding all other influences constant.

The law of demand describes a fundamental relationship between price and consumption. It states that, *ceteris paribus*, as the price of a good falls, the quantity demanded will increase. Conversely, as the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded will decrease. This inverse relationship is explained by two main effects: the **income effect** (a lower price increases the real purchasing power of a consumer's income, allowing them to buy more) and the **substitution effect** (a lower price makes the good relatively cheaper than other goods, encouraging consumers to switch their spending to it).

items: Inverse relationship: Price (P) ↑, Quantity Demanded (Qd) ↓ and P ↓, Qd ↑.
Based on the ceteris paribus assumption.
Underpinned by the income effect and the substitution effect.
This relationship results in a downward-sloping demand curve.

sections.question:
The following table shows Zara's weekly demand for cups of coffee from a local café. 

| Price (£) | Quantity Demanded |
|---|---|
| 4.00 | 1 |
| 3.50 | 3 |
| 3.00 | 5 |
| 2.50 | 7 |
| 2.00 | 9 |

(a) Using the data, draw and label Zara's demand curve for coffee. [2 marks]
(b) If the price of coffee falls from £3.00 to £2.50, identify the change in quantity demanded. [1 mark]
sections.solution:
(a) To draw the demand curve, plot the price on the vertical (y) axis and the quantity demanded on the horizontal (x) axis. Each pair of values from the table is a point on the curve. Connect the points to form a downward-sloping line or curve.

**Marking points:**
*   [1 mark] For correctly labelling the axes ('Price' or 'P' on the y-axis, 'Quantity Demanded' or 'Q' on the x-axis) and the demand curve ('D').
*   [1 mark] For accurately plotting the points and drawing a downward-sloping curve connecting them.

*(A correctly drawn and labelled diagram would show points (1, 4.00), (3, 3.50), (5, 3.00), (7, 2.50), and (9, 2.00) connected by a line.)*

(b) At a price of £3.00, the quantity demanded is 5 cups. When the price falls to £2.50, the quantity demanded increases to 7 cups. The change in quantity demanded is an increase of 2 cups per week.

**Marking points:**
*   [1 mark] For correctly stating the increase in quantity demanded is 2 cups.

sections.content: While individual demand is important, businesses and governments are usually more interested in **market demand**. This represents the total demand from all consumers for a particular good or service. To find the market demand curve, we simply add up the quantities demanded by every individual at each price level. This process is known as horizontal summation, because we are adding the quantities along the horizontal axis of the demand curve graphs.

sections.question:
The market for concert tickets consists of two consumers, Anya and Ben. Their individual weekly demand schedules are given below.

| Price (£) | Anya's Qd | Ben's Qd |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | 0 | 1 |
| 80 | 1 | 2 |
| 60 | 2 | 3 |
| 40 | 3 | 4 |

(a) Derive the market demand schedule. [2 marks]
(b) Draw the market demand curve, labelling it clearly. [2 marks]
sections.solution:
(a) To derive the market demand schedule, we sum the quantities demanded by Anya and Ben at each price level.

| Price (£) | Anya's Qd | Ben's Qd | Market Qd |
|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 0 | 1 | **1** (0+1) |
| 80 | 1 | 2 | **3** (1+2) |
| 60 | 2 | 3 | **5** (2+3) |
| 40 | 3 | 4 | **7** (3+4) |

**Marking points:**
*   [1 mark] For showing the method of summing individual quantities.
*   [1 mark] For calculating all market quantities correctly.

(b) The market demand curve is plotted using the Price and Market Qd columns. The curve will be downward sloping. It will also have a 'kink' if individuals enter the market at different prices. In this case, Anya only enters the market when the price is £80 or less.

**Marking points:**
*   [1 mark] For correctly labelled axes (Price, Quantity) and a title ('Market for Concert Tickets').
*   [1 mark] For accurately plotting the market demand curve using the derived data points (1, 100), (3, 80), (5, 60), (7, 40).

sections.content: Movements Along vs. Shifts of the Demand Curve

This is one of the most important distinctions in microeconomics. A **change in quantity demanded** is represented by a **movement along** the existing demand curve. This is caused *only* by a change in the price of the good itself. For example, a shop reducing the price of a chocolate bar causes a movement down along the demand curve for that chocolate bar.

A **change in demand**, however, is represented by a **shift** of the entire demand curve, either to the left (a decrease in demand) or to the right (an increase in demand). This means that at *every* price, consumers are now willing and able to buy more or less than before. A shift is caused by a change in any factor affecting demand *other than the good's own price*. These are called the non-price determinants of demand.

In exams, never say 'demand goes up' or 'demand goes down'. Be precise! Use 'quantity demanded increases/decreases' for a movement along the curve, and 'demand shifts to the right/left' for a shift of the curve. Always draw arrows on your diagrams to show the direction of a shift.
These are the factors that cause the entire demand curve to shift. A useful mnemonic to remember them is **PIRATES**:

items: **P**opulation: An increase in the number of consumers in a market will shift demand to the right.
**I**ncome: For normal goods, higher income shifts demand right. For inferior goods, higher income shifts demand left.
**R**elated goods (prices of): For substitutes, if the price of good A rises, demand for good B shifts right. For complements, if the price of good A rises, demand for good B shifts left.
**A**dvertising: Effective advertising and branding can increase consumer loyalty and shift demand to the right.
**T**astes and preferences: Changes in fashion, trends, or health consciousness can shift demand.
**E**xpectations: If consumers expect prices to rise in the future, current demand may shift right. If they expect prices to fall, current demand may shift left.
**S**easons: Demand for certain goods like ice cream, winter coats, or holiday decorations is affected by the time of year.

sections.content: When answering a question about a change in a non-price determinant, follow this structure: 1. Identify the determinant. 2. Explain how it has changed. 3. State the effect on demand (increase/decrease) and the direction of the shift (right/left). 4. Illustrate with a correctly labelled diagram showing the shift from D1 to D2.

items.label: Khan Academy: Demand, Supply and Market Equilibrium
